item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An amnesiac is somebody with amnesia. Amnesia is a nonspecific syndrome with multiple causes, there isn't a drug that "cures" it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It should be "amnestic" and not "amnesiac". An amnesiac is a medicine that cures amnesia.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The image in this article was removed because we could not properly determine whether or not the image was properly licensed or attributed. It may be added once again if/when proper attribution or proper use under CC guidelines is confirmed. Please contact anqxyr or another member of the Image Team if you have questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Take my upvote. Now I will run. Especially after reading the interview *shudder*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: jesus christ, what a hidden gem. Made me be squicked, +1 for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of my favorites so far +1. I think the 'demon' addition to the doll is a nice touch and nod to Japanese mythology surrounding birth. And I'm all for keeping the picture, it definitely adds to the mood.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was very nice and creepy.

The only bit I think could use more details is in the interview. It resumes to 'child looked very much deformed, looked at doctor, laughed at doctor, doctor snapped his neck'. Did everyone present see the child presumably stillborn, but laughing? Nurse is mentioned to have seen it but isn't specified what exactly she saw. Did everyone present consider it was alright that the doctor just went on and snapped the child's neck? Did seeing and touching the child affect the doctor in any way in his future?

I don't think this is something that leads people to think more about things, but is merely lack of details. And that's a thin line there. However, if this was intended, my apologies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I resolved to stay neutral on this when you showed this to me in the chat. But damn, that interview…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good point, thanks Dexa.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think radiation burns would be caused by yōkai from the 12th century. It's an interesting idea, but the established "Demons sealed in the doll from long ago" would contradict that effect. If it was a one-off incident? Sure.

Otherwise, i like the SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find myself pleasantly unsettled. Very good, sir. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And I'm going to black out the museum name, too. Might as well make that part even more ambiguous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm gonna go with Bright's suggestion on this one. :D

This part gets stream-of-consciousness, be warned. I've been thinking about how to flesh out the demonic portion of the file, and I think I'm going to go with witness interviews - since the object is Japanese and showed up in the '30s, an incident occurring right around Pearl Harbor or the Hiroshima/Nagasaki bombings feels right to me. A professor of Asian studies, helping pack away the Japanese collections in a time of highly anti-Japanese public sentiment? His research assistant, quite pregnant but still working on light duty, comes across the figurine, is affected, a doctor is called, she gives birth in a room at the museum… dies of blood loss during, horribly deformed infant (maybe what would later be recognized as radiation burns or post-bombing defects?) …I like the idea of it coming out and speaking before the doctor freaks out and kills it… something like this http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shikata_ga_nai , talking and laughing…

Any thoughts? :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one. Don't bother hiding the picture in any way. If you're looking at this site from work…

item.title: Re: author comment
item.content:encoded: For alot of work places leaving the fetus uncovered would still considered inappropriate and it loses alot without the full picture. Go with the click-down, maybe something about how the compulsion is transmitted by pictures of it as well, so it's hidden to protect female staff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Below commentor is correct - due to the risk of an unknown pregnancy, only those incapable of becoming pregnant are allowed to handle the SCP. Also, made your suggested fixes - thanks. :)

item.title: author comment
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback, everybody. I'm at work right now but I'll do some edits tonight - I agree with fleshing out the story somewhat, maybe putting in an incident report. I'm not sure how to handle the picture. It's NSFW but not, like, porn-y, and the article loses a lot of oomph without it - should I put it behind a click-through tag with a warning, or put some black censor boxes over the naughty bits?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't think it would be a good SCP but the concept is solid and the article is more-or-less well written. Just need some minor fixes here and there. The picture was off-putting for me at first but I think it's a great SCP photo. As I was reading, I was worried that the note that the incantation might've detracted from the article but since most of the incantation is illegible and since it's impossible to tell whether the SCP started screwing around with pregnancies before or after the original finders decided it was an evil spirit container, I don't think it's much of a problem.

+1

Edit: Probably needs a NSFW tag. Do we do nsfw tags on the wiki?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good thing nobody was looking over my shoulder when I opened this at work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn if you didn't find a good picture to work with…

Agreed with both of the above:
I wouldn't do any experiments with it… but it's begging for a pre-containment incident report.
The article doesn't give any reason that the average, non-pregnant female would be particularly threatened here.

Finally:
" and the characters are equal to a known early dialect of Japanese."

Imma put on my grammar Nazi cap for a minute… equal isn't quite right for this since it's usually used for quantities, it's like saying "they are equal in appearance".
I'd suggest: similar, identical, comparable, etc.
or even "and the characters have been identified as a known early dialect of Japanese."

Overall it's compelling, and when I first saw the picture I thought "oh sh*t, here we go". But the effects aren't quite compelling enough without hearing more about that demon…

EDIT:
"Baby we don't need to use that this time, I scheduled you to do some testing with 051 tomorrow."
-Dr. █████'s file titled "Things NOT to say to research assistants"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Most likely because a non-sterile female could be in the early stages (first few days, etc) of pregnancy without knowing it-They wish to thus make it impossible for a pregnant woman to come in contact with the SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The picture itself seems creepy…and you can remove it to show its insides? Ergh.

However, the article looks a little lacking. You might want to work on that, or explain the demons bit in the form of various incidents and whatnot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, now I'm curious. In the containment procedures, it states that any one can come into contact with this thing, except pregnant or non-sterile females. You explain WHY you shouldn't send a pregnant lady in, but you don't touch on the non-sterile females. Elaborate.

Also, the picture is captioned as 'Japanese Obstetrical Model'. Is it just a generic model? Or is it SCP-051? If it's SCP-051, then label it as such.

EDIT: Oh yeah, the title of the object is 601. Everywhere else is 051. You might want to change that.
